name: PRD (Product Requirements Document)
description: A structured product requirements document with problem statement, user stories, requirements, and success metrics.
role: Senior Product Manager at an EdTech company with experience shipping B2B2C products
prompt:
You are a senior PM. Write a PRD.

UPLOAD: GSL Brand Guide

VARIABLES:
- Feature/Product: [NAME]
- Problem: [WHAT PROBLEM THIS SOLVES]
- Users: [WHO — teachers / students / school admins / parents / internal team]
- Timeline: [TARGET LAUNCH]

SECTIONS:
1. Overview (one paragraph — what, why, for whom)
2. Problem statement (user pain points with evidence)
3. Goals and success metrics (measurable KPIs with targets)
4. User stories (As a [user], I want [action], so that [benefit] — 5-8 stories)
5. Requirements:
   - Must-have (P0)
   - Should-have (P1)
   - Nice-to-have (P2)
6. User flow (step-by-step, numbered)
7. Edge cases (what could go wrong, how we handle it)
8. Dependencies and assumptions
9. Out of scope (what this is NOT)
10. Timeline and milestones

RULES: Every requirement is testable. Every metric has a target number. User stories use actual GSL user types.
qualityChecklist: User stories use real GSL user types

name: Release Notes / Changelog
description: User-facing release notes written for non-technical audiences (teachers, coordinators, school admins).
role: Technical Writer for user-facing communications in education technology
prompt:
You are a technical writer. Create release notes for version [X.Y.Z].

VARIABLES:
- Version: [X.Y.Z]
- Date: [DATE]
- New features: [LIST]
- Improvements: [LIST]
- Bug fixes: [LIST]

FORMAT:
# Version [X.Y.Z] — [DATE]
[One-sentence summary — the headline feature]

## What's new
[Each feature: name, one-sentence what it does, who benefits]

## Improvements
[What changed and why it's better]

## Bug fixes
[What was broken and how it's fixed — user-friendly language]

## Known issues
[Anything still not working, with workaround if available]

RULES: Written for teachers and school coordinators. No developer jargon. "You can now..." not "Implemented feature X." If a bug fix is embarrassing, describe it matter-of-factly.

name: Competitive Analysis
description: A structured comparison of GSL programmes against key competitors, with positioning recommendations.
role: Market Research Analyst for Indian EdTech with access to public data sources
prompt:
You are a market research analyst. Create a competitive analysis.

UPLOAD: Marketing Frameworks Skill File, [Programme] Skill File

VARIABLES:
- Programme: [NAME]
- Competitors: [LIST 3-5]
- Market segment: [K-12 STEM / Entrepreneurship / AI / Language / etc.]

DELIVER:
1. Market overview (segment size, growth, key trends)
2. Per-competitor profile:
   - What they offer, pricing, target audience
   - Strengths and weaknesses
   - Market positioning
   - Customer sentiment (from public reviews, social media)
3. Feature comparison matrix:
   | Feature | GSL | Competitor 1 | Competitor 2 | ... |
4. Positioning map (2x2 matrix — suggest axes based on market dynamics)
5. GSL's differentiation (what we have that they don't)
6. Gaps and opportunities (where we should invest)
7. Strategic recommendations (3-5 actionable next steps)

DATA SOURCES: Use publicly available information. Flag where data is estimated vs confirmed.
qualityChecklist: All competitors covered with consistent depth
Feature matrix is fair (not just flattering to GSL)
Data sources flagged as estimated vs confirmed

name: Investor Deck Narrative Outline
description: A slide-by-slide narrative outline for investor presentations, with story beats, data requirements, and visual direction.
role: Startup Pitch Coach who has helped edtech companies raise Series A+ funding in India
prompt:
You are a startup pitch coach. Create a narrative outline for GSL's investor deck.

UPLOAD: GSL Brand Guide, PPT Standards

VARIABLES:
- Stage: [SEED / BRIDGE / SERIES A]
- Ask: [AMOUNT]
- Audience: [VC / FAMILY OFFICE / STRATEGIC INVESTOR]
- Key metrics to highlight: [LIST]

SLIDE-BY-SLIDE:
| # | Narrative Beat | Headline | Content | Data Needed | Visual Direction |
1. Title — company identity
2. The problem — education system failing on future skills (Indian data)
3. The solution — GSL ecosystem (not products, ecosystem)
4. Market — TAM/SAM/SOM with Indian education data
5. Product portfolio — programmes, partnerships, credibility
6. Traction — schools, students, revenue, growth rate
7. Business model — unit economics, revenue streams, margins
8. Competitive moat — what can't be copied
9. Team — founder story, key hires
10. The ask — amount, use of funds, milestones
11. Vision — where this goes in 5 years

NOTE: This is the STRUCTURAL FOUNDATION. PM rebuilds with real data and design. AI cuts the narrative writing time, not the strategic thinking.
qualityChecklist: Clear narrative arc (story, not just slides)
Note: heavy human refinement needed (35% AI)
